preamble: This phonetic transformation describes the historical shift where a voiced aspirated alveolar plosive becomes its voiceless aspirated counterpart. This change primarily involves the devoicing of the consonant, meaning the vocal cords cease to vibrate during its articulation, while the accompanying puff of air (aspiration) is retained.
phoneticEffects: Devoicing, Laryngeal shift (loss of voicing while retaining aspiration)

examples.note: The Classical Armenian and Eastern Armenian letter 'դ' (da), pronounced as a voiced alveolar plosive /d/, regularly corresponds to a voiceless aspirated alveolar plosive /tʰ/ in Western Armenian.
Proto-Indo-European voiced aspirated stops, including *dʰ, underwent devoicing to become voiceless aspirated stops like /tʰ/ in Ancient Greek.

sources: Armenian phonology - Paul Marciano Wiki - Fandom
Faliscan Reflexes of the Indo-European voiced aspirates - Wolfgang D. C. de Melo
Sound Changes of the Indo-European Stop Consonants - 2sh.me
